region_of_origin: India (Mughal / Awadhi tradition); served in Singapore

main_ingredients: whole spices (cinnamon, cloves, cardamom, bay leaf)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Dumpukht Pulao is approximately 400 g, typically served as a main course with raita or a side salad.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Rice Pulao / Pilaf composite; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for basmati rice, cooked chicken, and ghee
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Dumpukht Pulao. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN rice pulao composite data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked basmati rice (FDC ID 169704), roasted chicken (FDC ID 171477), and ghee (FDC ID 173410). The input record values (energy 162.5 kcal, protein 7.5 g, fat 6.25 g, carbohydrate 18.75 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 225 mg/100g is plausible for a salted, spiced preparation. All values represent a mixed meat-and-rice preparation at approximately 50% rice, 30% meat, 20% aromatics and fat by cooked weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Basmati rice, white, boiled (Atkinson et al., 2008); University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dumpukht Pulao as a composite dish. GI of 54 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white basmati rice, boiled (GI range 50–58, representative value 54). The presence of protein (7.5 g/100g from meat) and fat (6.25 g/100g from ghee) in the composite dish is expected to modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain basmati rice. GI of 54 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate for this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 75.0) / 100 = 40.5, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 18.75 × 4 = 75.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 225 mg/100g; 900 mg per standard 400 g serving, representing 45% of the 2000 mg/day recommended limit
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200 g or less per meal and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full 400 g serving delivers approximately 900 mg sodium — nearly half the daily recommended ceiling — with limited potassium or fibre offset from this dish alone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 18.75 g/100g with dietary fibre only 1.25 g/100g; a 400 g serving delivers 75 g total carbohydrate with GI ~54 and GL ~40.5
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict Dumpukht Pulao to a half-plate portion (approximately 150–200 g) to keep the glycaemic load below 20, and pair it with a high-fibre accompaniment such as cucumber raita or dal to blunt post-meal glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.0 g/100g from ghee; 12 g saturated fat per 400 g serving; trans fat 0.13 g/100g; sodium 225 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should choose the chicken variant over mutton to reduce saturated fat intake, request minimal ghee in preparation, and limit serving size to 200 g to keep saturated fat below 6 g and sodium below 450 mg per meal occasion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, cloves, saffron) is beneficial; however, red meat variant increases arachidonic acid load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis benefit from the anti-inflammatory spice profile of Dumpukht Pulao but should select the chicken variant over mutton to avoid the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid load associated with red meat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dumpukht Pulao is a fragrant, slow-cooked Indian rice dish prepared using the ancient Mughal 'dum pukht' technique, in which a sealed pot (traditionally sealed with dough) traps steam and allows the contents to cook in their own juices over low heat. The dish is built on long-grain basmati rice layered with partially cooked meat — most commonly chicken or mutton — and a rich base of caramelised onions, ginger-garlic paste, yogurt, ghee, and whole aromatic spices including cinnamon, cloves, green cardamom, bay leaf, and saffron. The slow, sealed cooking process produces rice grains that are perfectly separated, deeply fragrant, and infused with the flavours of the spices and meat juices. The texture is tender and moist rather than dry, and the aroma is distinctively floral from saffron and warm from the whole spices. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 162.5 kcal, 7.5 g protein, 6.25 g fat (of which 3.0 g is saturated, primarily from ghee), and 18.75 g carbohydrate with 1.25 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 225 mg/100g, making portion control important for hypertension management. The glycaemic index of the basmati rice base is approximately 54 (low GI), but the glycaemic load of a full 400 g serving is high at approximately 40.5, requiring portion moderation for type 2 diabetes. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is commonly served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and is associated with festive and celebratory occasions in South Asian culinary tradition.
